title: Whoever runs the agent owns the learning it compounds
summary: Glean's Arvind Jain and Osmantic's Ahmed Osman, on the same day, argued that the institutional learning an agent accumulates is an asset — held in markdown and context today, in weights tomorrow — that you either own or rent from the model provider.
takeaways: Treat the learning your agents accumulate as owned state: decide where the agent runs and where its memory lives before that knowledge compounds somewhere you don't control.
As agents do more of the work, the institutional know-how that used to live in your team accrues inside the agent, so not running it means renting both the labor and the compounding intelligence.
That learning sits in the context and markdown files agents read today and in fine-tuned weights tomorrow — portable, ownable state, but only on infrastructure you control.
sourceName: Glean's Arvind Jain (20VC) + Local AI Summit panel (Osmantic's Ahmed Osman)
body:
## What happened

In a [2026-07-11 20VC interview](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jX-Uq8JJ_j8), Glean founder Arvind Jain argued that as agents take over work, all that institutional learning "is actually going to accumulate in that agent that is doing that work" — and if you don't run the agent yourself, "you're basically fully dependent on the AI companies." The same day, on a [Local AI Summit panel](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KB41dTlX1Uc), Osmantic's Ahmed Osman named the mechanism: today's agents just "save to markdowns," and "the next one will be updating the weights, and that needs to happen locally."

## Why it matters

The work an agent does is visible; the *learning* it compounds is not. Every optimization your team once held in its head now accrues inside the agent — and if that agent and its memory run on someone else's infrastructure, you rent the compounding intelligence and can be cut off from it.

## How it works

1. **The learning is state.** It lives in the context, skills, and markdown files the agent reads today — and, Osman argues, in fine-tuned weights tomorrow.
2. **Markdown is a ceiling.** [Osman](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KB41dTlX1Uc) notes context length makes markdown memory inefficient at scale; weight-level updates are the successor, and they need local hardware.
3. **Ownership is a deployment choice.** [Jain](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jX-Uq8JJ_j8) frames it as control: run the agent where you own the memory, or accept operational dependence on the provider.
4. **It's why open and local pull enterprises.** Jain says more than ninety percent of enterprise use cases can now be *handled* by many models, open source included — GLM five point two was the first his own team trusted for the majority of their workloads. The pull is cost, as companies blow annual AI budgets in a month, and he expects most enterprise workloads on open models within three years.

> The learning an agent compounds is an asset — you either own the infrastructure it lives on or you rent it.

## The catch

Both are interested parties — an enterprise-AI founder and an open-source advocate — rather than neutral findings, and "own your weights" is aspirational: local continual learning is early, and one owned agent can cost more than the team it replaces (Glean's triage agent ran about a million dollars a month). Ownership is a real lever, but it isn't free.

[Memory & Context](/guide/memory-and-context/)
